lesson.title: Market Microstructure & Execution
lesson.body: The bid-ask spread is the difference between the best ask (lowest sell price) and best bid (highest buy price). Market impact is the price movement caused by a trade itself. TWAP (time-weighted average price) and VWAP (volume-weighted average price) are execution benchmarks that slice large orders over time to minimize impact. Limit orders reduce transaction costs but risk non-execution.

rooms.question: The bid-ask spread represents an immediate cost of:
answers: Half the spread per trade (for a round trip: the full spread)
Zero cost if using limit orders
rooms.explanation: Crossing the spread costs approximately half the spread per side (from mid-price). A round-trip buy+sell costs approximately the full spread.

rooms.question: Market impact refers to:
answers: Price movement caused by your own order (buying pushes price up, selling pushes it down)
rooms.explanation: Market impact: a large buy order consumes available liquidity at lower prices, pushing the execution price up. This is the primary cost for institutional-size orders.

rooms.question: VWAP execution aims to trade at the:
answers: Volume-weighted average price of the day, blending in with normal market volume
Best bid or ask at order entry
rooms.explanation: VWAP slices an order proportionally to expected intraday volume, aiming to achieve a price close to the day's volume-weighted average. It minimizes visible impact.

rooms.question: Order these execution methods from most aggressive (high impact) to least aggressive:
items: Market order: immediate execution at best available price
Limit order: passive, wait for price to come to you
rooms.explanation: Market orders are most aggressive (immediate impact). VWAP/TWAP algos spread impact over time. Dark pools hide size. Limit orders are most passive (no guaranteed fill).

rooms.question: Adverse selection in limit order trading means:
answers: Your limit orders get filled only when the price has moved against you
Market orders are cheaper than limit orders
rooms.explanation: Adverse selection: informed traders know when to 'pick off' limit orders. Your buy limit order gets hit when informed sellers know prices will drop further — you buy just before a decline.

boss.name: The Market Maker
boss.taunt: You pay my spread. I profit from your urgency. Every trade costs you.

questions.question: The Amihud illiquidity ratio = |R_t| / Volume_t measures:
answers: How much price moves per unit of trading volume (higher = less liquid)
questions.explanation: High Amihud ratio: small volume moves prices a lot → illiquid. Low Amihud: large volume needed to move prices → liquid. Used to screen for liquid tradeable assets.

questions.question: Implementation shortfall measures:
answers: The difference between the decision price and the actual executed price
questions.explanation: Implementation shortfall = (executed price − decision price) × shares. It captures all trading costs: spread, delay cost, market impact, and opportunity cost of unexecuted orders.

questions.question: For a quantitative strategy with high turnover, the most critical execution concern is:
answers: Transaction costs consuming alpha — strategies viable on paper may not survive real costs
questions.explanation: High-turnover strategies (daily rebalancing, mean reversion) can have alpha of 0.5-1.5 bps/day. A realistic bid-ask + impact cost of 1-2 bps can erase the entire edge.
